straight_beat.name: Eduardo 'Eddie' Solano
straight_beat.column:
The Dodgers lost yesterday, but not without a fight. Line by line, the game was a study in contrasts—clutch hitting, shaky relief, and a bullpen that couldn’t hold a lead. Texas didn’t just beat us; they did it with precision, patience, and a few timely singles that turned the tide. Let’s break it down.  

The first inning was a microcosm of the day. Walker Buehler, for all his dominance, walked the first two batters. That’s not a flaw in his stuff—it’s a flaw in the script. The Rangers’ hitters didn’t need to swing; they just needed to stay alive

optimist.name: Sunny Abramowitz
optimist.column:
The Dodgers lost yesterday, but let’s not pretend that loss is the story. The story is the way they fought, the way they kept their heads up, and the way their roster—this roster—still feels like a blockbuster in the making. You know what I’m talking about. The kind of movie where the underdog gets a second act, where the third quarter twist is just the setup for the finale. This team isn’t just built to win; it’s built to *break* records in October. Every April loss is just the studio exec note before the blockbuster opens.  

Let’s talk about the lineup. That’s where the magic happens. Corey Seager, the heart of the club, didn’t flinch. He’s the kind of player who turns a loss into a lesson, not a lament. And Mookie Betts? He’s out there swinging like he’s got a script written by a director who knows how to end a third act. The young guys—those rookies with the fire and the flair—they’re not just filling seats; they’re filling the narrative. You can see it in their eyes. They’re not worried about the standings. They’re worried about the next pitch, the next at-bat, the next chance to write their own ending.  

And let’s not forget the front office. They’re the ones behind the scenes, drafting the plot twists, making sure the story keeps turning. The way they’ve handled the draft, the trades, the free-agent signings—it’s all part of the script. They know the Dodgers aren’t just a team; they’re a franchise with a legacy. A legacy that’s been written in blue, and it’s still being written. The -1 game in the NL West? That’s not a hill to die on. It’s a stepping stone. A reminder that the season isn’t a straight line—it’s a curve, a loop, a story that’s got more chapters than a Hollywood blockbuster.  

You can’t win every game, but you can win the season. And the Dodgers? They’re not just playing to win. They’re playing to *dominate*. They’re playing to prove that even when the script calls for a setback, the real drama is in the comeback. So yeah, they lost yesterday. But if you’re looking for a reason to stay optimistic, look no further than the way they played. Look at the way they keep pushing forward, like they’re not just

pessimist.name: Marv Tannenbaum
pessimist.column:
The Dodgers lost again. Not because they’re bad, but because they’re the same team that’s been to every NLDS exit since Gibson’s homer. You know, the one that’s good enough to win 110 games but not good enough to win a playoff series. Regular season champs, yeah, but that’s just the warm-up act. The real test is November, and the bullpen’s already smoking a cigar outside the ballpark.  

Yesterday’s game against Texas was a textbook example of why we’re all still waiting for a playoff run. The Rangers didn’t just beat us; they exposed the cracks in our armor. We had a lead, we had momentum, and then—poof—gone. The bullpen, that ever-reliable group of guys who’ve been to every NLDS exit since Gibson’s homer, couldn’t hold a sniff. They’re supposed to be the last line of defense, but instead, they’re the first line
